run.objective: Establish a written Responsible AI policy for CS operations covering PII handling, human-review requirements, adoption standards, and impact measurement.
run.why: Run-stage teams have CSMs experimenting with AI individually: different tools, different practices, inconsistent outcomes. Some are pasting customer data into public LLMs. Some are sending AI drafts without review. Without a governance layer, you accumulate risk as adoption grows. A clear policy isn't about slowing AI adoption; it's about scaling it safely and measuring whether it actually improves outcomes.
steps: Audit current AI tool usage across the CS team: which tools, which use cases, how often, and what review practices CSMs follow today
Draft a PII policy: specify which customer data can be entered into AI tools, which tools are approved, and which are prohibited (e.g., no personal contact data into non-approved public LLMs)
Define human-review requirements by use case: customer-facing content requires CSM review before sending; internal summaries can be used as-is
Create a list of approved AI tools with their data processing terms verified by your legal or security team
Run a one-hour team training session covering: approved tools, PII rules, prompt hygiene, and how to catch hallucinations
Set an AI adoption baseline: what percentage of CSMs use AI tools weekly? Track this monthly going forward
Measure impact quarterly: time saved, accuracy of AI outputs, and whether AI-assisted interactions correlate with better customer outcomes
common_pitfalls: Writing a policy and filing it away. Governance only works if it's enforced and reviewed as tools evolve
Blocking all AI use in the name of safety. This pushes usage underground where you have even less visibility
Measuring AI governance by tool adoption alone. Adoption without outcome measurement tells you nothing about whether AI is helping or hurting

fly.title: CS-Led Revenue Strategy
fly.objective: Position CS as a strategic revenue engine with ownership of net revenue retention, expansion pipeline, and customer lifetime value optimization.
fly.why: At the fly stage, CS isn't just supporting retention; it's driving revenue. The best CS organizations own NRR as their north star and have direct influence on pricing, packaging, and go-to-market strategy. This playbook formalizes that shift: CS moves from a post-sale function to a strategic pillar of the revenue organization.
steps: Establish NRR as the primary CS metric with board-level visibility and quarterly targets by segment
Build a CS revenue model: forecast expansion, contraction, and churn by cohort and segment
Create a customer value optimization framework: identify which accounts have untapped potential and why
Embed CS in pricing and packaging decisions. CS has the closest view of how customers actually use and value the product
Develop a multi-year account planning process for strategic accounts with dedicated expansion roadmaps
Build CS-sourced pipeline reporting that's integrated with your CRM and revenue operations
Establish CS representation in board meetings and investor updates with NRR, expansion, and LTV narratives
common_pitfalls: Losing the customer advocacy mandate in pursuit of revenue. CS credibility depends on being the customer's champion, not another sales team
Measuring CS solely on revenue without retention guardrails. Expansion is meaningless if GRR is declining
Not investing in CS operations to support the revenue motion. You need RevOps-grade infrastructure for pipeline, forecasting, and attribution

fly.title: Predictive Customer Intelligence
fly.objective: Build an intelligence layer that anticipates customer needs, automates insight delivery, and enables proactive intervention at scale.
fly.why: Fly-stage organizations have the data volume, infrastructure, and operational maturity to move beyond reactive and even predictive models into genuine customer intelligence. This means not just predicting churn, but predicting expansion readiness, identifying product-market fit signals, surfacing cross-sell timing, and anticipating stakeholder changes, all automatically.
steps: Consolidate all customer data sources into a unified customer data platform: product usage, support, billing, CRM, engagement, sentiment
Build ML models for multiple prediction types: churn risk, expansion propensity, advocacy likelihood, stakeholder risk
Create an automated insight delivery system: CSMs receive prioritized, actionable intelligence daily, not raw data
Implement natural language processing on support tickets, call transcripts, and survey responses for sentiment trends
Build segment-level intelligence dashboards for CS leadership: cohort health, adoption trends, revenue forecasts
Establish a feedback loop where CSM actions inform model retraining. The system gets smarter as the team uses it
Publish customer intelligence insights to Product, Marketing, and Sales teams. CS becomes the organization's customer knowledge hub
common_pitfalls: Building intelligence capabilities without the data infrastructure to support them: garbage in, garbage out
Over-automating without human oversight. Models make mistakes, and customer relationships are too valuable for unchecked automation
Hoarding customer intelligence within CS instead of sharing across the organization. The value multiplies when Product, Sales, and Marketing can act on it too

fly.title: Embed CS Culture Company-Wide
fly.objective: Make customer success a company-wide operating principle, not a departmental function: every team owns customer outcomes.
fly.why: The final evolution of CS maturity is when customer success stops being a team and becomes a culture. Fly-stage organizations embed customer outcomes into every department's OKRs, every product decision, every sales conversation. When engineering cares about adoption, when marketing cares about expansion, when finance cares about LTV, that's when CS scales beyond headcount.
steps: Establish customer outcome metrics in every department's OKRs: Product owns adoption, Sales owns time-to-value, Support owns resolution impact on health
Create a cross-functional customer council that meets monthly: CS, Product, Sales, Marketing, Engineering leadership review customer health trends and act on them
Build a 'voice of customer' program that systematically routes customer feedback to the teams that can act on it
Embed customer success training in new hire onboarding for all departments. Everyone should understand the lifecycle, maturity model, and their role in it
Establish customer-centric decision frameworks: feature prioritization weighted by retention impact, marketing campaigns measured by expansion influence
Create shared dashboards and reporting that give every team visibility into customer health, adoption, and outcomes
Publish an annual 'State of the Customer' report internally: a data-driven narrative on customer health, trends, risks, and opportunities
common_pitfalls: Mandating culture change top-down without executive buy-in. It needs to be championed by the CEO, not just the VP of CS
Making it a one-time initiative instead of an ongoing operating rhythm. Culture requires reinforcement, not a workshop
Losing CS team identity in the process. CS should drive the customer-centric culture, not dissolve into it

fly.title: Deploy Agentic Workflows with Bounded Autonomy
fly.objective: Move beyond AI-assisted CSM work to bounded autonomous actions (tiered interventions, risk routing, and self-serve resolution) with explicit human oversight and measured business outcomes.
fly.why: Fly-stage teams have validated AI across their CS workflows. The next step is AI that acts, not just advises. Agentic workflows handle routine decisions at a scale no human team can match: automatically routing critical accounts to senior CSMs, triggering playbooks when health scores drop, or resolving tier-one support inquiries without CSM involvement. The key word is 'bounded': well-defined scope, explicit escalation triggers, and outcome measurement baked in from day one.
steps: Audit your current playbook triggers: which interventions are rule-based and repetitive? These are the first candidates for automation
Start with one bounded workflow: the most common high-confidence intervention (e.g., automatically assign a CSM to any account that drops below a health score threshold and has an upcoming renewal)
Define the escalation boundary explicitly: which account states require human decision-making versus automated action?
Build the workflow in your CS platform or automation layer, with every automated action logged and reviewable
Run the agentic workflow in 'shadow mode' for two to four weeks: it fires actions but a CSM approves each one before execution. This validates the logic before going autonomous
Move to autonomous execution only after shadow-mode accuracy exceeds your defined threshold (e.g., 90% of shadow actions would have been approved)
Track outcome metrics for AI-handled versus CSM-handled interventions (retention rates, response times, customer satisfaction). This is your business case for expanding scope
Establish a governance cadence: monthly review of agentic workflow performance, quarterly model reviews, and a clear rollback plan if outcomes degrade
common_pitfalls: Skipping shadow mode and going straight to autonomous. One bad automated action on an enterprise account is very expensive
Defining autonomy scope too broadly in v1. Start with one high-confidence, low-stakes workflow before expanding
Not measuring AI-handled versus human-handled outcomes. Without a control group you can't prove (or disprove) that agentic workflows improve results

trigger_signals: You have a reliable retention baseline (GRR tracked monthly for 3+ months)
Basic customer segmentation is in place and being used to differentiate engagement
At least one documented playbook exists (usually onboarding)
CSMs are spending more time on proactive outreach than reactive firefighting
Leadership asks about CS metrics beyond just churn count
AI copilots have been piloted and most CSMs use them weekly for call summaries and email drafts
key_shifts: From gut-feel to data-informed decisions: you start trusting health indicators over intuition
From one-size-fits-all to segmented engagement: different customers get different treatment
From individual heroics to repeatable processes: playbooks replace ad hoc actions
From tracking churn to tracking leading indicators: health scores, adoption, engagement
From no AI to AI-augmented CSMs: copilots reduce administrative work and free time for higher-value interactions
new_capabilities: Health scoring enables proactive risk intervention
Segmentation unlocks differentiated engagement models and resource planning
Lifecycle journey maps create predictability across the full customer lifecycle
QBR/EBR programs build executive relationships and surface expansion opportunities
CS begins contributing to retention narratives with data, not anecdotes
AI copilots standardize first drafts and meeting summaries, cutting prep and follow-up time across the team
risks: Tool overinvestment: buying a CS platform before your processes are defined leads to expensive shelfware
Metric proliferation: tracking 15 metrics when you can only act on 4 creates dashboard fatigue
Premature specialization: splitting CSM roles (onboarding vs. ongoing) before you have enough volume to justify it

trigger_signals: Health scores are established and CSMs use them daily to prioritize their time
Lifecycle journey maps exist and touchpoints are being followed consistently
GRR has stabilized or improved over 2+ quarters using your current playbooks
You're hitting CSM capacity limits. The current model won't scale to 2x accounts
Expansion revenue is happening but it's opportunistic, not systematic
AI-assisted health scoring has replaced or supplemented rule-based models with measurable accuracy improvements
key_shifts: From human-led to human-plus-digital: automation handles the repeatable, humans handle the strategic
From reactive risk management to predictive: you catch problems before they surface
From cost center to revenue contributor: CS demonstrates measurable impact on expansion
From departmental function to cross-functional partner: CS insights influence product, sales, and marketing decisions
From rule-based to ML-driven health scoring: predictions catch risk earlier with fewer false positives
new_capabilities: Digital engagement allows coverage of 10x more accounts without proportional headcount
Predictive churn models surface risk 30–60 days earlier than traditional health scores
Formalized expansion motion creates a predictable CS-sourced pipeline
Data infrastructure supports sophisticated segmentation, cohort analysis, and attribution
CS operations becomes a recognized function with dedicated resources
ML-driven health models identify at-risk accounts earlier and with higher accuracy than threshold-based rules
risks: Over-automating high-touch accounts: digital engagement is for scale segments, not your top 20 accounts
Expansion pressure without retention guardrails. If CSMs chase upsells while GRR declines, you're robbing Peter to pay Paul
Model complexity without interpretability: a churn model nobody understands won't change behavior

trigger_signals: Digital engagement is handling 60%+ of your customer base effectively
Predictive models are operational and CSMs trust and act on their outputs
CS-sourced expansion is a meaningful, tracked revenue stream (not one-off wins)
NRR consistently exceeds 110% and GRR exceeds 90% across segments
CS has a seat at the executive table and contributes to company strategy discussions
Agentic AI is handling bounded, autonomous interventions with governance oversight and measured outcomes
key_shifts: From revenue contributor to revenue driver: CS owns NRR and has direct influence on pricing, packaging, and go-to-market
From predictive to intelligent: models don't just predict risk, they recommend actions and anticipate needs
From CS department to CS culture: customer success becomes a company-wide operating principle
From operational to strategic: CS leadership shapes company strategy, not just executes it
From AI-augmented to AI-orchestrated: agentic systems execute bounded interventions autonomously, with humans overseeing outcomes rather than individual actions
new_capabilities: Customer intelligence platform provides automated, actionable insights across the entire customer base
CS-led revenue strategy influences product roadmap, pricing, and go-to-market decisions
Every department has customer outcome metrics in their OKRs
Advanced analytics (cohort analysis, LTV modeling, predictive expansion) run continuously and inform decisions in real-time
CS talent development pipeline produces future CS leaders and cross-functional customer advocates
Agentic workflows handle high-volume, low-stakes interventions autonomously, freeing CSMs to focus on strategic and complex accounts
risks: Losing customer advocacy in pursuit of revenue optimization. CS credibility depends on being the customer's champion
Technology dependence without human judgment: AI and automation are tools, not replacements for relationship intelligence
Complacency: fly-stage organizations can become bureaucratic and slow, losing the customer intimacy that got them there

glossary.description: Plain-language definitions of customer success terminology used across the framework. Each entry links to the page that covers the term in depth, where one exists.

terms.definition: The percentage of provisioned users or accounts that actively use a product within a defined window. A core indicator that the customer is realizing value, not just paying for access.
The share of customer issues resolved with AI involvement (suggestion, draft response, or full deflection). Measures how much CS leverage AI is producing, not how much AI is deployed.
The number of months of gross profit needed to recover the cost of acquiring a customer. Lower is better. Often owned by finance but watched by CS because retention extends payback economics.
Customer or revenue loss over a period. Logo churn counts customers; revenue churn counts dollars. Distinguishing the two is the first step to diagnosing the cause.
The first stage of CS maturity. Foundations being built, processes are ad hoc, success depends on individual heroics. The work is to install repeatable basics, not to optimize.
A short post-interaction survey measuring how satisfied a customer was with a specific touchpoint. Useful for spotting service quality drift; weak as a leading indicator of retention.
A survey item asking how much effort it took to get something done with the product or with support. Lower effort correlates more strongly with loyalty than satisfaction does.
The role responsible for a portfolio of customer accounts after the initial sale. The job is value realization, retention, and expansion, in that order.
The function that builds and maintains the systems CS runs on: tooling, segmentation, health scores, reporting, playbook tracking. Emerges as a distinct role in the walk and run stages.
The share of CSMs who use AI tools in their weekly workflow above a defined threshold. A leading indicator of whether AI investment is producing leverage or sitting unused.

A composite indicator that combines product usage, engagement, support signals, and other inputs into a single risk-or-strength rating per account. Useful for prioritization, not as the only retention metric.

The organizational pattern where retention and expansion are shared responsibilities across product, sales, marketing, and support. The opposite of CS-as-a-silo, where the CS team owns outcomes it cannot control alone.

Daily active users divided by monthly active users. A coarse stickiness measure; higher means the product is part of the user's daily routine. Less meaningful for products with naturally infrequent usage.
The frequency of customer issues that require executive or cross-functional intervention. Rising escalation rate is often a leading indicator that health scores are missing something.
Additional revenue from existing customers through upsell, cross-sell, or seat expansion. The component of NRR that pulls retention above 100%.
Expansion revenue expressed as a percentage of starting recurring revenue over a period. The growth-from-existing-customers half of NRR.

The fourth stage of CS maturity. Customer outcomes are predicted, automation handles low-stakes work, and CS shapes company strategy rather than reacting to it. Risks shift from execution to complacency.
Recurring revenue retained from existing customers, excluding expansion. Caps at 100%. Tells you how leaky your bucket is before any growth is layered on top.
The percentage of customers retained over a period, ignoring deal size. A counterweight to revenue retention: a healthy NRR with falling logo retention often hides concentration risk.
One of four levels (Crawl, Walk, Run, Fly) describing where a CS organization stands across structure, process, data, and impact. Stage governs which playbooks and metrics to prioritize.

Recurring revenue retained from existing customers including expansion, net of churn and contraction. The single most-quoted CS health metric for SaaS. Above 100% means existing customers are growing faster than they are leaving.

The structured period after purchase during which a customer reaches first value. The boundary between onboarding and ongoing CS is when the customer has demonstrably achieved the outcome they bought the product for.
The percentage of customers who finish a defined onboarding milestone within a target window. A leading indicator of retention; customers who stall in onboarding rarely recover.
A documented, repeatable response to a specific customer situation: onboarding, renewal risk, expansion opportunity, escalation. Playbooks turn individual judgment into team-level consistency.
How many of a product's high-value features each account actively uses. Width matters less than depth: customers using 3 features deeply churn less than those using 10 features shallowly.
A scheduled review with a customer covering value delivered, outcomes achieved, and the path forward. Quarterly is conventional; the right cadence depends on segment and contract size.
The third stage of CS maturity. Operations are scaled and measurable, segmentation is multi-factor, digital and human touch are blended deliberately. The work shifts from execution to optimization.

The elapsed time from contract signature to the customer reaching the outcome they bought the product for. Different from time to first login or time to onboarding completion, both of which are easier to measure but mean less.
The elapsed time from contract signature to the customer experiencing a first meaningful value moment, ahead of full outcome realization. A useful early signal in long onboarding cycles.

The second stage of CS maturity. Process is forming, segmentation exists, basic health scoring is in place. The work is to standardize what worked at smaller scale and remove the dependency on heroics.
